meta.title: C216 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes CL C216 engine — M273, M278, M156, M157, M275. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and V12 AMG explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes CL C216 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C216 engine typically costs £3,500–£18,000 supply only, depending on engine — the M273 5.5-litre V8 sits at £3,500–£6,000, while the M275 V12 in the CL 65 AMG is the most expensive at £11,000–£18,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on engine complexity and specialist rates. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes CL C216 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M278 4.7-litre twin-turbo V8 (CL 500, 435 PS) — introduced at the 2010 facelift — is widely considered the most reliable engine in the C216 range. It offers strong performance, improved efficiency, and fewer of the M273's camshaft adjuster issues. The M273 is proven but sensitive to camshaft adjuster wear and intake manifold issues.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes CL C216?

acceptedAnswer.text: Camshaft adjuster and intake manifold failure on the M273 V8 (pre-facelift), head bolt and camshaft adjuster issues on the M156 AMG V8 (CL 63 AMG), ignition coil and ABC suspension failure on the M275 V12 (CL 600, CL 65 AMG), and cooling system issues on the M157 twin-turbo V8 (facelift CL 63 AMG). The ABC suspension is a common non-engine failure across all variants.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes CL C216 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reliability varies significantly by engine. The M278 (facelift CL 500) and M157 (facelift CL 63 AMG) are the most modern and generally more reliable than the pre-facelift M273 and M156. The M275 V12 is robust but the ignition system and ABC suspension are common failure points. The C216 is a high-end grand tourer — maintenance costs are commensurate with its original £100,000+ price tag.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes CL C216 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M156 6.2-litre V8 (pre-facelift CL 63 AMG) has documented head bolt and camshaft adjuster issues that can be extremely expensive to address. The M275 V12 (CL 600, CL 65 AMG) is also costly to maintain — coil packs, ignition modules, and the ABC suspension are all expensive failure points. If you're on a budget, the M278-powered CL 500 is the sweet spot. Avoid any car without documented service history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C216 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle on the M273 or M156 (camshaft adjusters), loss of power or rough running on the M273 (intake manifold), coolant/oil mixing on the M156 (head bolts), misfires on the M275 (coil packs/ignition modules), or persistent coolant loss on the M157 (cooling system) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes CL C216 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value and condition. Against a well-kept £25,000 CL 500, a £4,000–£7,000 recon is 16–28% of the car's value — viable. Against a tired £10,000 CL 500, the same recon is 40–70% — borderline. For a CL 65 AMG, a £40,000+ car justifies a £10,000–£18,000 recon; a £25,000 example makes it a much harder call. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line — but remember the C216's ABC suspension and other systems can add significantly to ownership costs.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes CL C216 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M278 twin-turbo V8 can exceed 150,000 miles with regular servicing and prompt camshaft adjuster attention. The M273 V8 is similarly durable but requires camshaft adjuster attention around 60,000–100,000 miles. The M156 and M157 AMG V8s can last 150,000+ miles but require significant maintenance along the way. The M275 V12 is robust but the ignition system requires regular attention.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C216 CL and the C217 S-Class Coupé?

acceptedAnswer.text: The C216 (2006–2014) is the last-generation CL-Class — a standalone grand tourer built on the W221 S-Class platform. The C217 (2014–2021) is the S-Class Coupé, which replaced the CL nameplate entirely. The C217 shares the W222 S-Class platform and offers more modern engines, including the M177 V8 and M256 straight-six. Engines are generally not interchangeable between the two generations.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes CL C216 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (under the bonnet or on the driver's door pillar), on the engine block, and in the vehicle documentation. Tell us your variant (CL 500, CL 550, CL 600, CL 63 AMG, CL 65 AMG) and approximate model year (pre-2010 or post-2010 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes CL C216 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C216 coupé specifically — including pre-facelift M273/M156/M275 units and post-facelift M278/M157 replacements.

overview.intro: The C216 is the last-generation Mercedes-Benz CL-Class, the flagship grand tourer produced from 2006 to 2014 as the successor to the C215. Built on the W221 S-Class platform, it combined luxury coupé styling with S-Class technology. The engine range was exclusively petrol — spanning M273 5.5-litre V8s, the M278 4.7-litre twin-turbo V8 introduced at the 2010 facelift, M156 and M157 AMG V8s, plus M275 5.5-litre and 6.0-litre twin-turbo V12s in the CL 600 and CL 65 AMG. The C216 was replaced in 2014 by the C217 S-Class Coupé.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W221 (S-Class) • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz CL-Class (C215) • Successor: Mercedes-Benz S-Class Coupé (C217) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (CL is a standalone model).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C216 CL-Class generated UK engine enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA], with the M273 V8 and M275 V12 remaining the most commonly discussed among specialists.

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode — one paragraph: The C216's most dependable engine is the M278 4.7-litre twin-turbo V8 (CL 500, 435 PS) — introduced at the 2010 facelift, it offers strong performance, improved efficiency, and fewer of the M273's camshaft adjuster issues. The M273 5.5-litre V8 (CL 500, 388 PS) is the pre-facelift workhorse — proven but sensitive to camshaft adjuster wear and intake manifold issues. The M275 V12 in the CL 600 (517 PS) is a masterpiece of engineering — effortless torque, silky refinement, but eye-watering maintenance costs when the coil packs, ignition modules, or ABC suspension fail. The M156 6.2-litre V8 in the CL 63 AMG (525 PS) is the last of the naturally aspirated AMG V8s — a genuine modern classic with a thunderous soundtrack, but head bolt issues and camshaft adjuster wear are well-documented. The M157 twin-turbo V8 in the facelift CL 63 AMG (571 PS) offers more power and torque but shares the hot-vee cooling system challenges of other M157-powered AMGs. Avoid any pre-facelift M273 without documented camshaft adjuster replacement, and budget for ABC suspension maintenance on all C216s.

rows.ownershipVerdict: NA V8 Icon — epic soundtrack, epic bills
Turbo V8 — more power, same caveats
Performance Pack — the one to have

rows.commonMajorFailure: Coil packs, ignition modules, ABC suspension

rows.ownershipVerdict: V12 Ultimate — the pinnacle, at a price

economicsRule.title: The C216 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,000–£7,000 recon on a sub-£15,000 C216 sits at 27–47% of the car's value — a serious decision. Against a well-kept £25,000+ example, the same recon drops to 16–28% — viable. For a CL 65 AMG, a £40,000+ car justifies a £10,000–£18,000 recon; a £25,000 example makes it a much harder call. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line — but remember the C216's ABC suspension and other systems can add significantly to ownership costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C216 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CL 500 (M278) — the most modern and reliable V8, best fuel economy.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C216 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CL 65 AMG (M275) — V12 complexity, twin-turbo maintenance, eye-watering parts prices.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C216 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: CL 500 (M278) — 435 PS, twin-turbo V8, facelift improvements, without V12 or AMG running costs.

problems.title: M273 V8 Camshaft Adjuster & Intake Manifold Failure
problems.rootCause: The M273's camshaft adjusters can wear prematurely, causing a rattle on cold start and, in severe cases, timing chain stretch. The intake manifold's swirl flaps can also fail, causing a loss of power and, in some cases, engine damage if debris enters the cylinders.

tieredCosts.work: Replace camshaft adjusters and intake manifold
Full timing chain and engine rebuild

problems.recommendation: If your CL 500 has a rattle on cold start or a loss of power, have the camshaft adjusters and intake manifold inspected immediately — £1,000–£1,800 is far cheaper than the £3,500+ bill that follows if the timing chain stretches or debris enters the engine. Use the correct oil spec and avoid extended service intervals.

cta.label: Compare M273 engine prices for your C216 CL 500 →

problems.title: M156 AMG V8 Head Bolt & Camshaft Adjuster Issues
problems.rootCause: The M156 6.2-litre V8 is prone to head bolt failure — the original bolts can stretch or break, causing coolant and oil to mix. Camshaft adjuster wear is also common, causing a rattle on cold start. The 2010 facelift's M157 addressed these issues but introduced its own challenges.

tieredCosts.work: Replace head bolts with updated items, camshaft adjusters
Full engine rebuild if head gasket has failed

problems.recommendation: If your CL 63 AMG has any signs of coolant loss or oil contamination, have the head bolts inspected immediately — a £1,500–£2,500 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £8,000+ rebuild that follows. Budget for this as a preventative job at 50,000 miles if you plan to keep the car long-term.

cta.label: Compare M156 engine prices for your C216 CL 63 AMG →

problems.title: M275 V12 Ignition Coil & ABC Suspension Failure
problems.rootCause: The M275 V12's ignition system — particularly the coil packs and ignition modules — is prone to failure, causing misfires and reduced power. The ABC (Active Body Control) suspension system on all C216s is also a common failure point, with leaks in the hydraulic lines, struts, and pump being expensive to repair.

tieredCosts.work: Replace coil packs and ignition modules
Full engine management system overhaul + ABC repairs

problems.recommendation: If your V12 is misfiring or running rough, have the ignition system inspected immediately — £1,000–£1,800 is far cheaper than the £4,000+ bill that follows if the catalytic converters are damaged. For the ABC system, have the hydraulic fluid and lines inspected at every service — a £600–£1,200 preventative repair is a fraction of the £2,000+ bill if the pump fails.

cta.label: Compare M275 engine prices for your C216 CL 600/CL 65 AMG →

problems.title: C216 ABC (Active Body Control) Suspension Failure
problems.affectedModels: All C216 CL-Class variants (standard ABC suspension).
problems.rootCause: The C216's ABC hydraulic suspension system — including struts, accumulators, hydraulic lines, and the pump — is prone to leaks and failures. The system operates at high pressure (up to 200 bar), and a single leak can cause the entire system to fail, leaving the car sitting on its bump stops.

tieredCosts.work: Replace accumulators and check hydraulic lines
Full ABC system overhaul including pump and struts

problems.recommendation: Have the ABC system inspected at every service after 60,000 miles — £800–£1,500 in preventative work is a fraction of the £3,000+ bill if the pump fails and damages the valve block. Listen for the pump running excessively or any signs of hydraulic fluid leaks under the car.

cta.label: Compare C216 ABC suspension repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: C216 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are C216 coupé only. There were no diesel or hybrid variants offered in the CL-Class range. The C216 was produced from May 2006 to 2014, with a facelift in 2010. The CL 550 was the RHD (UK) designation for the CL 500.
